food_subgroup: Cooked fish dishes with spice-based sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for 'Pepper Fish' as a prepared Indian spiced fish dish. Mapped to broader 'fish dish' class (FOODON_03302051) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — cooked fish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers cooked fish broadly. No specific code exists for Indian black pepper fish preparation. Broader category used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific prepared Indian fish dish. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Pepper Fish is approximately 200 g, comprising boneless fish pieces coated in a black pepper and spice masala, served as a main dish alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, rohu, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked fish with spices for fat fractions
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides the closest approved primary source for cooked Indian fish dishes. Values for rohu (a common boneless fish used in Indian restaurant preparations) cooked with spices were used as the primary anchor. Black pepper, curry leaves, and oil additions were factored in using standard Indian recipe composition. Sodium was estimated from typical restaurant-level seasoning. Fat fractions (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated) were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked fish (NDB 15264). The input record description mentions 'boneless chicken cubes' which is inconsistent with the food name 'Pepper Fish'; the fish identity is treated as authoritative based on food_name and food_group.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on cooked fish with spices (low-carbohydrate protein-dominant dish)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian Pepper Fish. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous low-carbohydrate cooked fish dishes documented in Atkinson et al. (2021) International GI Tables. Cooked fish dishes with spice coatings and minimal starch are consistently classified as low-GI foods (GI < 40) in peer-reviewed GI literature. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (30 × 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 2.28, rounded to 2.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from restaurant-level seasoning and salt
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving of Pepper Fish contributes approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 37–42% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content supporting glycemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pepper Fish has a low estimated GI of 30 and provides 17.5 g protein per 100 g, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable omega-3 fatty acid profile from fish and low saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pepper Fish provides a beneficial polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio with 2.1 g polyunsaturated fat and only 1.4 g saturated fat per 100 g, supporting cardiovascular health; however, restaurant sodium levels warrant monitoring for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pepper Fish is a popular Indian spiced fish dish in which boneless fish pieces — commonly rohu, pomfret, or kingfish — are marinated and sautéed with black pepper, curry leaves, green chillies, onion, garlic, and ginger in oil. The dish is characterised by a bold, pungent black pepper heat balanced by the aromatic fragrance of curry leaves, producing a dry or semi-dry preparation with a firm, slightly charred exterior. It is widely served in North and South Indian restaurants across Singapore, where it appears on menus as a main course alongside rice, roti, or paratha. Nutritionally, Pepper Fish is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with 17.5 g protein and 7.2 g fat. The fish base contributes omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. The glycemic index is estimated as low (GI ≈ 30), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary clinical concern is sodium content at restaurant preparation levels, which may be elevated for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Note: the input seed record contained an erroneous description referencing 'boneless chicken cubes'; the food identity has been corrected to fish based on the canonical food name 'Pepper Fish' and the food group 'Fish and seafood'.
common_user_queries: Is pepper fish good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact match for 'Pepper Fish' as a prepared dish was found in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo databases. Nutrient values were adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked fish (rohu) data with adjustments for spice and oil additions typical of Indian restaurant preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary source (ICMR-NIN) is an approved regional source and the adaptation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. The input seed record description contained an internal inconsistency: it described 'boneless chicken cubes tempered with curry leaves and green chillies & sauteed in black pepper' while the food_name is 'Pepper Fish' and food_group is 'Fish and seafood'. The fish identity has been treated as authoritative and the description corrected accordingly. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on ICMR-NIN cooked fish data; exact values will vary by fish species, oil quantity, and restaurant preparation. 3. Sodium is estimated at restaurant-preparation levels and may be higher than home-cooked equivalents. 4. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Indian Pepper Fish was identified in the University of Sydney GI Database or Atkinson et al. (2021). 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for non-hydrogenated cooking oil preparations; this is naturally zero for fresh fish cooked in vegetable oil without industrial processing. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are mapped to broader category anchors as no specific ontology term for Indian Pepper Fish exists in current releases.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food identity from erroneous chicken description to fish based on food_name and food_group.
Added multilingual local names in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.
Populated full nutrient profile adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked fish data.
Added GI analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (2021) peer-reviewed journal with GL calculation.
Mapped ontology to broader FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors with honest gap notes.
Wrote 1,050+ character LLM-ready natural language description.
Added all three required health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Built comprehensive aliases list with 17 entries and verified consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases.
Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to comply with allowed enum values, resolving G5 hard gate failure.
